coredata.dc:title: Significantly enhanced electrochemical performance of 2D Ni-MOF by carbon quantum dot for high-performance supercapacitors
coredata.dc:creator: Pan, Yi
Yan, Sai
Liu, Yuqing
Tian, Zhen
Li, Dan
Chen, Yanjun
Guo, Li
Wang, Yanzhong
coredata.dc:description: Rational design of metal-organic framework (MOF) materials with rich active sites and high conductivity is very important for their applications in the energy storage devices, but it is still a great challenge. Herein, carbon quantum dot (CQD)/NiMOF composites are synthesized via a one-pot hydrothermal method. The experimental results show that CQDs can effectively enhance the electrical conductivity of NiMOF and regulate its morphology to expose abundant active sites, which are beneficial for electrochemical performance. The as-prepared CQD@MOF composite delivers a specific capacitance of 2457.9 F g−1 at 1 A g−1, and the outstanding cycling stability with the capacitance retention of 84.5% after 5000 charge/discharge cycles. Moreover, the hybrid supercapacitors with activated carbon (AC) as the negative electrode and CQD@MOF as the positive electrode exhibits a wide voltage window of 1.7 V and a high energy density of 67.1 Wh kg−1 at a power density of 5.1 kW kg−1. The results can effectively boost the electrochemical performance of MOF and promote its application in supercapacitor electrode materials.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Supercapacitors
MOF
Binder-free
CQD
Hybrid supercapacitor

Program for the Outstanding Innovative Teams of Higher Learning Institutions of Shanxi

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The work was financially supported by the Fundamental Research Program of Shanxi Province (Nos. 20210302123067, 20210302124643 and 202103021224181), and Program for the Outstanding Innovative Teams of Higher Learning Institutions of Shanxi. The authors would like to thank Shiyanjia Lab ( www.shiyanjia.com ) for the support of XPS and TEM tests.

The work was financially supported by the Fundamental Research Program of Shanxi Province (Nos. 20210302123067, 20210302124643 and 202103021224181), and Program for the Outstanding Innovative Teams of Higher Learning Institutions of Shanxi. The authors would like to thank Shiyanjia Lab ( www.shiyanjia.com) for the support of XPS and TEM tests.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the synthesis process of CQD@MOF-0.5.
SEM of (a) pristine MOF, (b) CQD@MOF-0.25, (c) CQD@MOF-0.5, (d) CQD@MOF-1, (e, f, g) TEM and HRTEM image of CQD@MOF-0.5.

(a) XRD patterns of pristine MOF, CQD@MOF-0.25, CQD@MOF-0.5, and CQD@MOF-1, (b) The Raman spectrums of pristine MOF and CQD@MOF-0.5, (c) XPS survey spectrum, high-resolution XPS spectra of (d) Ni 2p, (e) O 1s, (f) C 1s.

(a) CV curves at a scan rate of 30 mV s−1, (b) CV curves of CQD@MOF-0.5 at the scan rate of 5 -100 mV s−1, (c) the curves of the peak current i and the square root of the scan rates v 1/2, (d) The curves of logi vs. logv, (e) the capacitive contribution at 100 mV s-1, (f) the percentage of capacitance contribution at diverse scan rates, (g) the GCD curve of CQD@MOF-0.5, (h) the capacity at different current densities calculated from GCD curves, (i) Nyquist plots, (j) Bode plots, (k) the cyclic stability tested at 10 mA g−1, inset initial and last 10 cycles.
Schematic illustration of the storage mechanism of CQD@MOF-0.5.

Electrochemical performance of the CQD@MOF-0.5//AC: (a) Schematic diagram of HSC, (b) CV curves of CQD@MOF-0.5 and AC in a three-electrode system at 30 mV s−1, (c) CV curves of HSC at 30 mV s−1 in diverse voltage windows, (d) CV curves of the HSC at diverse scan rates at 0 - 1.7 V voltage window, (e) GCD curves of HSC at diverse current density, (f) the capacity at diverse current densities calculated from GCD curves, (g) Nyquist plots, (h) the cyclic stability of HSC at 10 A g-1 for 10000 cycles, (i) Ragone plots, (j) Practical application of solid-state HSC.

head.ce:title: Significantly enhanced electrochemical performance of 2D Ni-MOF by carbon quantum dot for high-performance supercapacitors

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, North University of China, Taiyuan 030051, P. R. China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aSchool of Materials Science and Engineering, North University of China, Taiyuan 030051, P. R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Advanced Energy Materials and System, North University of China, Taiyuan 030051, P. R. China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Advanced Energy Materials and System

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bInstitute of Advanced Energy Materials and System, North University of China, Taiyuan 030051, P. R. China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Rational design of metal-organic framework (MOF) materials with rich active sites and high conductivity is very important for their applications in the energy storage devices, but it is still a great challenge. Herein, carbon quantum dot (CQD)/NiMOF composites are synthesized via a one-pot hydrothermal method. The experimental results show that CQDs can effectively enhance the electrical conductivity of NiMOF and regulate its morphology to expose abundant active sites, which are beneficial for electrochemical performance. The as-prepared CQD@MOF composite delivers a specific capacitance of 2457.9 F g−1 at 1 A g−1, and the outstanding cycling stability with the capacitance retention of 84.5% after 5000 charge/discharge cycles. Moreover, the hybrid supercapacitors with activated carbon (AC) as the negative electrode and CQD@MOF as the positive electrode exhibits a wide voltage window of 1.7 V and a high energy density of 67.1 Wh kg−1 at a power density of 5.1 kW kg−1. The results can effectively boost the electrochemical performance of MOF and promote its application in supercapacitor electrode materials.

Carbon quantum dot (CQD)/NiMOF composites are synthesized via a one-pot hydrothermal method, in which CQDs can effectively enhance the electrical conductivity of NiMOF and regulate its morphology to expose abundant active sites.

ce:section.ce:para: Supercapacitors (SCs) have been regarded as promising energy storage devices owing to their merits of high power density [1], fast charge/discharge kinetics [2], and a long lifetime of charge/discharge [3]. In practical applications, supercapacitors are usually classified into the symmetrical and hybrid capacitors according to the charge storage mechanism of electrode materials [1]. The symmetrical capacitors store charges via an electrostatic accumulation at the electrode/electrolyte interface, resulting in an ultra-high power density and long cycling life [4]. However, the symmetrical capacitors suffer from relatively low energy density, which limits their practical applications [2]. The hybrid supercapacitors (HSCs) are constructed by using the pseudocapacitive material as a positive electrode and electric double-layer capacitive material as a negative electrode, which can broaden the voltage windows, resulting in the high energy density [5]. Therefore, exploring high-performance pseudocapacitive electrodes is crucial to obtain high-performance HSCs [6].

Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) have attracted tremendous attention in supercapacitor fields because of their diverse structures, ultra-large specific surface areas, and porous structure [7]. However, MOFs have the drawback of low electronic conductivity in practical applications. Therefore, most MOFs are utilized as a template to prepare different nanomaterials including carbons, metal compounds, or their hybrid composites. Although this method can effectively avoid the inherent shortcomings of MOFs, the ordered pore structure of MOFs is inescapably destroyed, thus reducing the electrochemical active surface area [8]. Therefore, enhancing the electronic conductivity of MOFs without damaging their porous structure is the key to the direct employment of MOFs as supercapacitor electrodes.

Many strategies have been utilized to boost the electronic conductivity of MOFs in the supercapacitor field. One solution is to design and synthesize new MOFs with high electrical conductivity. Sheberla et al. [9] reported that the graphene-like π-conjugation structure Ni3(2,3,6,7,10,11-hexaiminotriphenylene)2 (Ni3(HITP)2) possesses the efficient overlap among the metal ions and ligands frontier orbitals, resulting in high electronic conductivity. However, Ni3(HITP)2 exhibits the electrochemical double-layer capacitive capacitance of 102 F g−1, which is even lower than that of activated carbons. Another strategy is to hybrid conductive materials for boosting the electrical conductivity of MOFs, such as carbon materials (CNT, acetylene black, graphene, etc.), conducting polymers (polypyrrole and polyaniline), and other conducting materials (MXene, metal sulfide, and metal selenides) [10]. For instance, Liu et al. [11]. introduced the conductive polypyrrole nanotube into NiCo-MOF, and the composites exhibit the high specific capacitance of 1109 F g−1 at 0.5 A g−1 and excellent cycling stability, which is attributed to the fast ion and electron transfer kinetics. Wang et al. [12]. reported that 2D NiCoMOF/MXene hybrid nanosheets with high electronic conductivity were prepared via a facile ultrasonic method, and the specific capacitance of composites is up to 815 F g−1 at 1 A g−1. In general, the introduction of conductive materials into MOFs can not only enhance their electronic conductivities but also avoid the serious aggregation. However, the conductive materials generally possess the lower specific capacitance, and they would also block the contact between the active sites and electrolyte, thus undermining the electrochemical properties of MOFs. Therefore, the introduction of the conductive nanomaterials into MOFs is a practical and effective method to improve the capacitive performance of MOFs.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are zero-dimensional carbon material with sp2 hybridization, which has attracted great attention in potential application, such as bio-imaging, catalysis, fluorescent probing, and energy storage due to their small size, homogeneous distribution, rich functional groups, lower cost, and easy fabrication [13]. CQDs have rich oxygen groups with negative charges, which can enhance the wettability and modulate the morphologies of composites. Furthermore, CQDs can construct electron-rich regions to change the partial electronic environment, thus enhancing the electronic conductivity of electrodes [14]. Benefiting from the above merits, CQDs can improve the cycling life and coulomb efficiency of electrode materials [15]. For instance, Song et al. [16] synthesized Mn-Co LDH@carbon dots, demonstrating that carbon dots can enhance the density of states nearby the Femi level to optimize the electronic conductivity as well as the electrochemical performance of Mn-Co LDH. Wei et al. [17]. synthesized the sea urchins-like, chestnut-like, flower-like, and bayberry-like CQDs/NiCo2O4 composites via adjusting CQD dosage, and the optimal CQDs/NiCo2O4 composites displayed the specific capacitance of 2168 F g−1 at 1 A g−1 and exceptional the capacitance retention of 75% at 30 A g−1. Therefore, CQD can not only boost the electrical conductivity of pseudocapacitive materials but also optimize their morphologies. As we know, the hybrids of MOFs and CQD as supercapacitor electrode materials are rarely reported.

Herein, we synthesize the flower-like hybrids of CQDs and NiMOF (CQD@MOF) on the nickel foam (NF) via a one-pot hydrothermal method. The results show that CQDs can enhance the electrical conductivity of pristine MOFs and modulate their morphologies to expose abundant electroactive sites. The as-prepared CQD@MOF-0.5 exhibits the high specific capacitance of 2457.9 F g−1 at 1 A g−1, and the excellent cycling stability with 84.5% retention after 5000 charge/discharge cycles. Furthermore, the assembled CQD@MOF-0.5//AC hybrid supercapacitor (HSC) achieves the maximum energy density of 67.1 Wh kg−1 at a power density of 5.1 k W kg−1, displaying the promising potentiality of practical application.

In a typical experiment, NiCl3·6H2O (0.75 mmol) and p-phthalic acid (PTA, 0.75 mmol) were dissolved into the mixed solution of DMF (30 mL), ethanol (3.5 mL), CQD solution (0.5 mL), and deionized water (3 mL). The mixed solution was transferred into a 50 mL Teflon-lined autoclave with a treated NF (2 cm × 3 cm), and heated at 140 °C for 14 h. The CQD@MOF loaded NF was washed with DMF, deionized water, and ethanol sequentially. The CQD@MOF/NF was dried at 60 °C overnight, named CQD@MOF-0.5. For the comparative experiment, the electrode materials with x mL CQDs solution (x=0, 0.25, and 1) were prepared by using the same experimental condition with CQD@MOF-0.5, which are named as pristine MOF, CQD@MOF-0.25, and CQD@MOF-1, respectively.
The preparation process of CQD solution and characterization of samples see the supporting information presented.

The preparation process of CQD@MOF-0.5 is schematically displayed in Fig. 1 a. Firstly, CQD was fabricated via an electrochemical exfoliation of the graphite rod in deionized water. As shown in Fig. S1, the solution of the as-prepared CQD with different concentrations demonstrates a good dispersion, which facilitates the uniform dispersion of CQD in NiMOF. Secondly, the CQD/MOF hybrid was synthesized via a one-pot hydrothermal method. The microstructures of samples are examined by scanning electron microscopy (SEM). Fig. 2 a shows that the pristine NiMOF demonstrates 2D nanosheet-like structure, which is uniformly grown on the NF. After introducing CQD, the morphologies of NiMOF have changed significantly. As shown in Fig. 2b-c, the thickness of NiMOF nanosheets slightly decreases, and the space between nanosheets obviously becomes larger with increasing the CQD concentration, which facilitates the immersion of electrolyte ions. When the concentration of CQD increases up to 1 mg ml−1, Fig. 2d shows that the sporadic ultrathin nanosheets are dispersed on the surface of NF. In general, CQD has abundant hydroxyl and carbonyl functional groups with negative charges, which can easily adsorb Ni2+ via the electrostatic force, resulting in the local nucleation and growth to form the flower-like MOF [18]. Additionally, the excess CQDs would suppress the growth of NiMOF on NF [17]. The morphology of CQD@MOF-0.5 is further examined by the transmission electron microscope (TEM). The TEM images (Fig. 2e) further confirm the nanosheet microstructures of CQD@MOF-0.5 with few layers. Comparing with the amorphous structure in the HRTEM image of pristine MOF (Fig. S2), the HRTEM image of CQD@MOF-0.5 (Fig. 2f and g) exhibited the crystalline nanoparticles with the diameter of 5-10 nm on the surface of NiMOF, and the fringe lattices spacing is 3.2 Å, which matched well with (002) plane of carbons [19], indicating the hybridization of CQD with NiMOF.

The crystal structures of samples were analyzed by X-ray diffraction (XRD). In Fig. 3 a, all samples exhibit similar XRD patterns, and the peaks at 8.8, 16.2, 17.4, and 29.2o are indexed to the (100), (10-1), (110), and (30-1) planes of NiMOF phase (CCDC 638866) [20], indicating that CQD with low contents has slight effect on the crystal structure of MOF. However, CQD@MOF-1 with high CQD contents exhibits weak XRD patterns, indicating a low crystallinity. The Raman spectra are displayed in Fig. 3b. For the spectrum of pristine NiMOF, the three peaks located at 1140, 1440, and 1617 cm−1 correspond to the C-H bond, and the peak at 826 cm−1 was assigned to the C-O-Ni bond [21]. Comparing with pristine NiMOF, CQD@MOF-0.5 shows similar peaks affiliated with C-H and C-O-Ni bonds, indicating the existence of NiMOF in CQD@MMOF-0.5. The peaks located at 1354 and 1617 cm−1 correspond to D and G bands of carbons, respectively, demonstrating the presence of CQD [22]. The results show that CQD has slight effect on the crystal structure of MOFs. The FT-IR of pristine Ni MOF and CQD@MOF is performed to determine the functional groups. As shown in Fig. S3, the pristine NiMOF and CQD@MOF-0.5 display the similar functional groups. The stretching peaks of 1370 and 1580 cm−1 are indexed to -COO− ions stretching vibrations, and the stretching peaks at 749 and 832 cm−1 are ascribed to C-H stretching vibrations, which originate from ligand. The peak at 3590 cm−1 corresponds to -OH stretching vibrations of water in MOF. Compared with CQD@MOF-0.5, the peak at 1640, 1720, and broad peak at 3420 cm−1 are indexed to C=C, C=O, and -OH stretching vibration, respectively. Compared with CQD@MOF-0.5, the peak at 1640, 1720 and broad peak at 3420 cm-1 are indexed to C=C, C=O and -OH stretching vibration [23], respectively, which is similar to CQD, indicating the existence of CQD in CQD/MOFs. [24].

To further gain insight into the elemental composition and chemical state of pristine NiMOF and CQD@MOF-0.5, the XPS measurement was performed. The strong peaks of Ni 2p, C 1s, and O 1s are observed in the XPS survey spectrums (Fig. 3c). The high-resolution Ni 2p profiles (Fig. 3d) of CQD@MOF-0.5 are fitted into the two main peaks at 855.8 and 873.4 eV corresponding to Ni 2p3/2 and Ni 2p1/2, respectively, in which the spin energy difference of 17.6 eV is matched with NiMOF [25]. The weak peaks in 861.5 and 879.5 eV were ascribed to satellite peaks. Compared with pristine MOF, the Ni 2p3/2 and Ni 2p1/2 peaks of CQD@MOF-0.5 were slightly positively shifted 0.3 and 0.4 eV, respectively, indicating that CQD can modulate the electronic structure of NiMOF. The O 1s spectra (Fig. 3e) of CQD@MOF-0.5 demonstrate that two main peaks located at 531.6 and 533.4 eV are assigned to O-Ni and O=C-O, respectively [26]. Compared with pristine NiMOF, the peak of O-Ni in CQD@MOF-0.5 shifted slightly negative by 0.2 eV, indicating that the electron environment of O atoms has slightly changed. The C 1s spectra (Fig. 3f) of CQD@MOF-0.5 and pristine MOF can be deconvoluted into the peaks centered at 284.4 eV (C-C/C=C), 285.8 eV (C-OH), and 288 eV (C=O) [27]. The element contents (Fig. S4) from XPS of pristine MOF demonstrate that the atomic percentage of C in CQD@MOF-0.5 is 57.8 at. %, which is slightly higher than that of pristine MOF (54.9 at. %), indicating the successful hybrid of CQD with NiMOF.

The electrochemical performances of specimens were measured in a three-electrode system. Fig. 4 a shows that CV profiles of all samples measured at 30 mV s−1 exhibit the obvious redox peaks at 0.2 - 0.5 V, indicating the typical pseudocapacitive behavior. The Faradic redox reactions correspond to the subsequent Eqs. (1) and (2) [28]:
(1)
N i
( I I )
s
+ O
H −
↔ N i
( I I )
( O H )
a d
+
e −
(2)
N i
( I I )
+
( O H )
a d
↔ N i
( I I I )
( O H )
s
+
e −

Among all CV curves, CQD@MOF-0.5 exhibits the maximum integrated area, indicating that CQD@MOF-0.5 possessed the highest specific capacitance. Moreover, all CQD@MOF electrodes show a narrower peak potential of redox reaction than that of pristine MOF, implying that the electronic conductivity of CQD@MOF can be improved by the addition of CQD. To further examine the electrochemical properties of CQD@MOF-0.5 at different scan rates, CV curves measured at scan rates from 5 to 100 mV s−1 are shown in Fig. 4b. The shape of redox peaks maintain the superb symmetry, indicating CQD@MOF-0.5 has the exceptional reversibility of redox reaction. In addition, the redox peaks slightly shift the more negative and positive potentials with improving scan rates, respectively, which is assigned to the polarization due to the internal resistance [29].

The diffusion of electrolyte ions in the internal electrode materials is evaluated by using the Eq. S5 [30] Fig. 4c shows that the square root of scan rate (v1/2 ) is proportional to the peak current (ip ) [31], which matches well with the characteristic of battery-type materials. CQD@MOF-0.5 shows the maximum slop, indicating the superior ion diffusion kinetics [32]. According to the Eq. S5, the value of Ip/v1/2 (CQD@MOF-0.5):Ip/v1/2 (pristine MOF) is 1.48, and thus the ion diffusion coefficient of CQD@MOF-0.5 is 2.2 times of pristine NiMOF, indicating that the hybrid of NiMOF with CQD is profitable for enhancing ion diffusion kinetics.

To identify the charge storage mechanism of CQD@MOF-0.5, the relationship of the redox peak current (i) and the corresponding the scan rates (v) can be evaluated by using the following Eq. (3) [33]:
(3)
i =
a
v b
where, a and b present constants. The linear relationship between log (i) and log (v) can provide the value of b. Typically, b =1 presents the surface capacitive-controlled charge/discharge process, while b=0.5 indicates diffusion-controlled process. Fig.5d shows that the b-values of reduction and oxidation reactions of CQD@MOF-0.5 are 0.5 and 0.48, respectively, suggesting the semi-infinite diffusion-controlled charge/discharge process. In addition, the percentages of capacitive contributions in divergent scan rates can be evaluated by using Eq. (4) [34]:
(4)
i
( V )
=
k 1
v +
k 2
v
1 / 2
where, i represents a current density at the potential of V, k1 v and k2 v1/2 is the current contribution in diffusion-controlled and capacitive-controlled processes, respectively [33]. According to Eq. (4), the capacitive contribution of CQD@MOF-0.5 is identified and shown as the shaded distinct of CV profiles at 100 mV s−1 in Fig.5e. The calculated results (Fig. 4f) show that the percentage of the capacitive contribution is 35.2% at 5 mV s−1, and the value increases up to 70.4% at 100 mV s−1, which would contribute to the admirable rate capability [35].

The GCD curves of CQD@MOF-0.5 and pristine MOF at different current densities are shown in Fig. 4g and Fig. S5, respectively. The typical platforms of charge/discharge are observed, further confirming the typical characteristic behavior of battery-type electrodes. The low IR drop of GCD curves displays the high electronic conductivity of CQD@MOF-0.5. The specific capacitances of specimens are calculated and expressed into Fig. 4h. The gained specific capacitance of CQD@MOF-0.5 achieved up to 2457.9 F g−1 at 1 A g−1, which exceeded pristine NiMOF (1545.9 F g−1). The capacitance retention of CQD@MOF-0.5 remains 68.1% even at 40 A g−1, exceeding those of pristine MOF, CQD@MOF-0.25, and CQD@MOF-1. To further evaluate the electrical conductivity and diffusion kinetics of CQD@MOF-0.5, EIS measurements were performed, and the obtained Nyquist plots are shown in Fig. 4i. The intercepted x-axis at the high-frequency part servers as the equivalent series resistance (Rs ), and the quasi-semicircle in middle-frequency regions represents charge-transfer resistance (Rct ). [36] It can be seen that the values of Rs and Rct decrease gradually with increasing CQD contents, which further confirms that CQD is favorable to enhance the electrical conductivity of NiMOF. The values of Rs and Rct of CQD@MOF-0.5 are 0.75 and 6.3 Ω, respectively, which is lower than those of pristine MOF (Rs =1.1 Ω and Rct =13.6 Ω). The Warburg impedance (Zw) of CQD@MOF-0.5 was relative to the inclined line in the low-frequency region. The value of Zw slightly decreases with increasing CQD contents, demonstrating the superior ion diffusion kinetics at the interface between electrolyte and electrodes. The bode profiles are expressed in Fig. 4j. The phase angle of CQD@MOF-0.5 is 62o, which exceeds the pristine NiMOF (51.7o), revealing that CQD@MOF-0.5 possesses superior diffusion kinetics compared with pristine NiMOF [37]. The cyclic stabilities of pristine NiMOF and CQD@MOF-0.5 are displayed in Fig. 4h. The capacitance retention of CQD@MOF-0.5 remains 84.5% afterward 5000 charge/discharge cycles, which is much higher than that of pristine NiMOF (77.5%).

As shown in Fig. 5, the excellent electrochemical properties of CQD@MOF-0.5 are assigned to the subsequent aspects: (i) the open structures of CQD@MOF-0.5 are prominent for exposing extra active sites of MOFs along with immersing in electrolyte, efficiently utilizing active sites of MOF; (ii) CQD can effectively enhance the electronic conductivity of MOFs, accelerating charge transfer; (iii) CQD@MOF-0.5 is in situ grown on the NF for directly employing as supercapacitor electrode materials, and the three-dimensional structure and excellent electronic conductivity of NF are favorable to the ions and electrons transfer at the interface of electrode/electrolyte. Therefore, the above unique structural characteristics boost the specific capacity, rate performance, and cyclic stability of CQD@MOF-0.5.

To gain insight into the practical application of CQD@MOF-0.5, Fig. 6 a shows the assembled CQD@MOF-0.5//AC hybrid supercapacitor (HSC) by using CQD@ MOF-0.5 as a cathode and active carbon (AC) as an anode, respectively. The cellulose paper and 2 M KOH are used as a separator and an electrolyte, respectively. Fig. 6b shows CV profiles of CQD@MOF-0.5 and AC at 30 mV s−1 in a three-electrode system. The CV curve of AC exhibits a typical rectangle shape in the potential range of -1 - 0 V, and the redox peaks are observed in the CV curve of CQD@MOF-0.5 in the potential range of 0 - 0.6 V, indicating the typical behaviors of electric double-layer capacitance and pseudocapacitance, respectively. CV profiles (Fig. 6c) were tested at 30 mV s−1 to distinct the voltage window of CQD@MOF-0.5//AC. The typical polarization is observed at 1.8 V. Accordingly, the suitable voltage of CQD@MOF-0.5//AC is adopted in 1.7 V. Fig. 6d shows the CV curves of HSC measured at 5 - 100 mV s−1 in the voltage range of 0 - 1.7 V, in which all CV curves exhibit the similar shape, indicating the excellent reversibility even at a high scan rate. The GCD curves of HSC are demonstrated in Fig. 6e, which exhibits the charge/discharge platform, approximately symmetrical shape, and low IR drop, suggesting the excellent electrochemical performance of HSC. The specific capacitances of CQD@MOF-0.5//AC HSC calculated from GCD curves (Fig. 6f) are 167.2, 155.8, 141.7, 110.2, 88.7, 70.6 and 49.0 F g−1 at 5, 7, 10, 20, 30, 40 and 50 A g−1, respectively. The Nyquist profiles of CQD@MOF-0.5//AC (Fig. 6g) demonstrate the low R s, R ct and Zw, revealing the prominent electronic conductivity along with fast diffusion kinetics of ions. Fig. 6h shows that the capacitance retention of HSC remains 83.9% after 10000 charge/discharge cycles, indicating the outstanding cycling stability. The wave shape of capacitance retention may be caused by the following reasons: (i) at the initial cycles, the increased capacitance is attributed that CQD@MOF-0.5 is activated during the charge/discharge process, revealing extra active sites; (ii) the reduced capacitance is caused by the fact that the structure and morphologies of electrode materials may be destroyed after many charge/discharge cycles. The obtained Ragone plot of HSC is Fig. 6i. The maximum energy density of HSC reaches up to 67.1 Wh kg−1 at a power density of 5.1 kW kg−1, which is more prominent to the results of previous reports, such as porous NiMOF//AC (21.1 Wh kg−1 into 6000 W kg−1) [38], NiMOF/CNTs//rGO/g-C3N4 (36.6 Wh kg−1 at 480 W kg−1) [11], hexagonal NiMOF//AC (55.3 Wh kg−1 into 362.5 W kg−1) [28], CoMnMOF//AC (30 Wh kg−1 at 2285.7 W kg−1) [39], NiCoMOF//AC (49.4 Wh kg−1 at 562.5 W kg−1) [27], cuboid NiMOF//AC (31.5 Wh kg−1 at 800 W kg−1) [40]. Moreover, the practical applications of CQD@MOF-0.5//AC HSC are exhibited in Fig. 6j, which can power the temperature-humidity sensor for 12 min (S video 1) and the LED bulb (S video 2) for 6 min, indicating the promising application as electrode materials for high-performance supercapacitors.

To sum up, CQD@NiMOF hybrid materials on nickel foam (NF) were successfully synthesized via a one-pot hydrothermal method, direct using as supercapacitor electrode materials. CQD as a conductive agent can boost the electronic conductivity of NiMOF. Furthermore, CQD can modify the morphology of NiMOF nanosheets for revealing extra active sites. The specific capacitance of CQD@MOF-0.5 reaches up to 2457.9 F g−1 at a current density of 1 A g−1, exceeding the pristine NiMOF. The capacitance retention of CQD@MOF-0.5 can maintain 84.5% after 5000 charge/discharge cycles, indicating the outstanding cyclic stability. The assembled hybrid supercapacitor exhibits the maximum energy density of 67.1 Wh kg−1 at a power density of 5.1 kW kg−1. The excellent electrochemical property of CQD@MOF displayed the promising potentiality as high-performance supercapacitor electrode materials.

Yi Pan: Methodology, Investigation, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Sai Yan: Methodology, Investigation. Yuqing Liu: Methodology, Data curation. Zhen Tian: Data curation, Validation. Dan Li: Methodology, Resources. Yanjun Chen: Resources, Investigation. Li Guo: Funding acquisition, Supervision. Yanzhong Wang: Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work was financially supported by the Fundamental Research Program of Shanxi Province (Nos. 20210302123067, 20210302124643 and 202103021224181), and Program for the Outstanding Innovative Teams of Higher Learning Institutions of Shanxi. The authors would like to thank Shiyanjia Lab (www.shiyanjia.com) for the support of XPS and TEM tests.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.electacta.2022.140560.
